paper_title: Switch-KD: Visual-Switch Knowledge Distillation for Vision-Language Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in joint vision-language understanding, but their large scale poses significant challenges for deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Knowledge Distillation (KD) offers a viable way to improve model capabilities without increasing model size or data requirements, making deployment more efficient. However, applying KD to VLMs is challenged by modality-specific supervision: although multimodal knowledge in VLMs is fused within the language space, current methods supervise each modality separately without explicitly addressing multimodal alignment, leading to inconsistent multimodal knowledge transfer. To address this, we propose Switch-KD, a visual-switch distillation framework that unifies vision-language knowledge transfer within a shared text-probability space. Switch-KD comprises two key components: (1) Visual-Switch Distillation, which switches the student's visual outputs into the teacher's language pathway to construct cross-modal probabilistic references for implicit visual knowledge transfer; and (2) Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, which adaptively aligns informative probability regions while preserving the distributional structures of teacher and student through bidirectional supervision. Guided by Switch-KD, a 0.5B TinyLLaVA effectively distills rich multimodal knowledge from its 3B teacher, yielding an average improvement of 3.6 points across 10 multimodal benchmarks without any architectural modification.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Switch-KD: Visual-Switch Knowledge Distillation for Vision-Language Models Haoyi Sun*, Xiaoxiao Wang, Ning Mao, Qian Wang, Lifu Mu, Wen Zheng, Tao Wei, Wei Chen Li Auto Inc. 6 2 0 2 6 1 ] . [ 1 9 2 6 4 1 . 4 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in joint visionlanguage understanding, but their large scale poses significant challenges for deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Knowledge Distillation (KD) offers viable way to improve model capabilities without increasing model size or data requirements, making deployment more efficient. However, applying KD to VLMs is challenged by modality-specific supervision: although multimodal knowledge in VLMs is fused within the language space, current methods supervise each modality separately without explicitly addressing multimodal alignment, leading to inconsistent multimodal knowledge transfer. To address this, we propose Switch-KD, visualswitch distillation framework that unifies visionlanguage knowledge transfer within shared text-probability space. (1) VisualSwitch-KD comprises two key components: Switch Distillation, which switches the students visual outputs into the teachers language pathway to construct crossmodal probabilistic references for implicit visual knowledge transfer; and (2) Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, which adaptively aligns informative probability regions while preserving the distributional structures of teacher and student through bidirectional supervision. Guided by Switch-KD, 0.5B TinyLLaVA effectively distills rich multimodal knowledge from its 3B teacher, yielding an average improvement of 3.6 points across 10 multimodal benchmarks without any architectural modification. Our code and project page are publicly available. 1. Introduction Vision-language models (VLMs) [1, 30, 44] exhibit exceptional performance in joint vision-language understanding, driven by large-scale data and substantial computational resources. Their observed performance improvements closely follow the scaling law. However, the substantial latency, memory footprint, and energy cost of large VLMs limit their *Corresponding author: sunhaoyi@lixiang.com. Figure 1. Radar chart comparing the performance of Switch-KD0.5B, LLaVA-KD-0.5B [2], and TinyLLaVA-0.5B [56] over five competency dimensions. Switch-KD consistently outperforms the other two models, demonstrating its comprehensive and superior capabilities. applicability in resource-constrained or real-time scenarios, motivating the need for lightweight alternatives. Knowledge Distillation (KD) [4, 12, 14, 16, 55] offers well-established paradigm for compressing model scale by transferring knowledge from high-capacity teacher to lightweight student, enabling efficient deployment with minimal accuracy degradation. While KD has achieved notable success in text-only language models [9, 19, 26, 48, 54], extending it to VLMs remains challenging. As multimodal knowledge in VLMs is inherently fused within the language space, effective knowledge distillation should likewise transfer cross-modal information through the shared text-probability space. widely adopted and effective approach, logits distillation [11, 16, 17, 41, 55], minimizes the divergence between teacher and student textprobability distributions, serving as strong language-side supervision. As natural extension, recent methods have begun to explore supervision on the visual modality alongside the language side. For instance, Align-KD [7] explicitly aligns respective LLM attentions and respective visual tokens, while LLaVA-KD [2] enforces consistency between the LLM-generated visual tokens and their corresponding logits. However, these approaches lack tight integration between the visual and language components, employing nonunified supervision across modalities. This non-unified supervision overlooks the intrinsic coupling between modalities, thereby limiting the efficiency of multimodal knowledge transfer. To address these challenges, we introduce Switch-KD, unified distillation framework that integrates visual and language knowledge transfer within shared text-probability space. Switch-KD adopts novel Vision-Switch Distillation design, switching the students visual outputs into the teachers language pathway and supervising them with the Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, which adaptively aligns teacherstudent distributions via bidirectional Top-k rank matching to enable implicit visual knowledge transfer. As key component of Switch-KD, Visual-Switch Distillation integrates two complementary pathways, standard alignment pathway and visual-switch pathway, both of which align studentteacher output logits entirely within the shared text-probability space. The standard pathway follows the conventional approach, matching the students logits with those of the teacher. In contrast, the visual-switch pathway switches the students visual encoder outputs into the teachers language decoder (S-ViT T-Projector TLLM) to produce visual-switch logits, which represent the teachers output distribution conditioned on the students encoded visual representations. This alignment within the shared text-probability space implicitly enables the student to learn from the teacher at the visual-encoder level, achieving visual knowledge transfer through the language pathway. Effective alignment in the shared text-probability space must address the long-tailed logits of LLMs, as they hinder efficient student imitation during distillation. BiLD (Bidirectional Logits Difference) [26] addresses this issue by bidirectionally aligning the pairwise differences between the teachers and students top-k logits. However, fixed is incapable of adapting to the different logits distributions observed across different models and samples. To overcome this limitation, we propose Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, designed to dynamically select the most informative logits for distillation. Specifically, we locate the transition point between the information-rich and long-tail regions and adopt it as the adaptive top-k boundary. Based on this adaptive threshold, DBiLD performs bidirectional top-k alignment between teacher and student logits differences via reverse KL divergence, focusing on high-confidence regions to achieve more stable and effective knowledge transfer. Our method surpasses both state-of-the-art VLM distillation methods and existing lightweight VLM approaches across wide range of multimodal benchmarks, as shown in Fig. 1. We summarize our main contributions as follows: Unified text-probability distillation framework. We propose Switch-KD, novel distillation framework that unifies multimodal supervision within shared textprobability space, ensuring consistent knowledge transfer between teacher and student models. Visual-switch distillation with DBiLD loss. We develop novel visual-switch distillation architecture that implicitly transfers visual knowledge through the visual-switch pathway, and propose the Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, which adaptively emphasizes informative logits via an adaptive top-k boundary and performs bidirectional alignment with reverse KL divergence. Comprehensive empirical validation. Extensive experiments on 10 multimodal benchmarks show that SwitchKD consistently surpasses existing distillation methods and significantly improves the students visual encoder across diverse tasks. 2. Related Work advances Vision-Language Models: Recent in visionlanguage models (VLMs) have driven rapid progress in multimodal understanding through increasingly unified Early works such architectures [22, 23, 42, 4547]. as CLIP [36] aligned visual and textual modalities via large-scale contrastive learning, while BLIP-2 [24] bridged frozen vision encoder and an LLM using lightweight Q-Former. Recent models [1, 30, 44] commonly adopt ViTProjectorLLM design with instruction tuning for multimodal understanding. Scaling analyses [20, 34, 35] demonstrate that VLM performance scales consistently with increases in model size, data volume, and computational budget. However, in resource-constrained or real-time scenarios, large VLMs suffer from computation and latency bottlenecks, making lightweight alternatives increasingly essential. Recent efforts [28, 29, 50, 56] thus explore compact and efficient architectures that retain competitive performance under limited resources. Building on these advances, Knowledge Distillation (KD) [12, 40, 43, 49] has emerged as promising approach to further enhance the performance of lightweight multimodal models. Knowledge Distillation For VLMs: KD for large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable progress, with most studies [9, 19, 26, 48, 54] focusing on designing advanced loss functions to effectively transfer textual knowledge from the teacher to the student model. HowFigure 2. Overview of the proposed Switch-KD framework, consisting of two components: (a) Visual-Switch Distillation (left), where the students visual outputs are switched into the teachers language pathway to obtain visual-switch logits for implicit multimodal knowledge transfer; and (b) DBiLD loss (right), which first detects knee points kt and ks in the respective logits distributions, then constructs two sets of logits differences and bidirectionally aligns them via reverse KL divergence, with an emphasis on high-confidence regions for stable and effective knowledge transfer. ever, extending KD to VLMs remains challenging due to the additional complexity introduced by the visual modality. Existing multimodal KD approaches for VLMs can be broadly classified into three categories: (1) Architectureenhancement methods. LLaVA-MoD [38] and MoVEKD [3] extend the student model with Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) structures [15], aiming to improve knowledge transfer through sparse parameter activation. (2) Intermediatelayer supervision methods. VLsI [21] introduces layer-wise verbalizers to align the student with the teachers reasoning trajectory. While effective, such designs substantially increase architectural complexity and training cost. (3) Explicit visual-constraint methods. Align-KD [7] aligns the first-layer textvision attention of the language model, implicitly aligning visual tokens from structurally matched visual encoder. LLaVA-KD [2] aligns the self-correlation of LLM-generated visual tokens and additionally enforces logit-level consistency for these tokens . While explicit visual supervision facilitates visual knowledge transfer, such modality-separated designs limit the overall distillation efficacy. Going beyond these approaches, our proposed Switch-KD enables collaborative distillation of cross-modal knowledge entirely within unified text-probability space, ensuring coherent modality-integrated supervision while preserving architectural efficiency. 3. Method Prior work on multimodal knowledge distillation often treats visual features or visual-modality logits as separate supervision targets, leading to fragmented and inconsistent knowledge transfer. We revisit this problem from unified probabilistic perspective and ask: if all modalities can be aligned within shared language probability distribution, can such alignment enable more unified cross-modal knowledge transfer in place of modality-specific supervision? Building on this insight, we propose Switch-KD, unified framework that combines Visual-Switch Distillation for implicit visual knowledge transfer and Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss for adaptive probabilistic alignment, as illustrated in Fig. 2. 3.1. Visual-Switch Distillation As shown in the left of Fig. 2, the overall process of visualswitch distillation consists of two forward pathways: the standard alignment pathway and the visual-switch pathway. The overall training objective of the distillation framework is given by: = LCE + λ1LAlign + λ2LVSD, (1) where LCE denotes the auto-regressive language modeling loss, and λ1 and λ2 are balancing coefficients that control the relative contributions of the standard alignment distillation term LAlign and visual-switch distillation term LVSD. Both pathways involve teacher model = (V , , LT ) and student model = (V S, S, LS). Each model follows the same modular structure: visual encoder , projector , and language model L. Standard Alignment Pathway. Given an input image xv and text prompt xt, the teacher generates its logits zT R1N through the standard forward process: zT = (xv, xt) = LT (P (V (xv)), xt), (2) where denotes the vocabulary size of language model LT . Similarly, the student model generates the corresponding logits zS R1N as: zS = S(xv, xt) = LS(P S(V S(xv)), xt). (3) During distillation, the teacher is frozen while the student is fully trainable. We minimize the distributional difference between zT and zS as: LAlign = LDBiLD(zT , zS), (4) where LDBiLD(, ) represents the general distribution alignment metric detailed in Section 3.2. This pathway conducts overall supervision in the language space, with comparatively less supervision for the visual modality, motivating the introduction of the visual-switch pathway. Visual-Switch Pathway. To complement the overall textprobability space supervision, the visual-switch pathway explicitly leverages the switch mechanism of for more direct and effective visual knowledge transfer. The core hypothesis is that if the student visual encoder learns meaningful representations, its visual representav = S(xv) should be correctly interpreted and detions hS coded by the teachers language pathway (P , LT ), yielding probability distribution consistent with the teachers original output. Based on this intuition, we defined the visual-switch logits zSwitch R1N as follows: zSwitch = LT (P (V S(xv)), xt), (5) where the output of the students visual encoder is switched into the frozen teacher projector and language model to form hybrid inference route, representing the teachers output distribution conditioned on the students visual representations hS . To further facilitate implicit visual knowledge transfer in the shared text-probability space, we minimize the visualswitch logits discrepancy with the teacher logits: LVSD = LDBiLD(zT , zSwitch). (6) Intuitively, this process can be viewed as hypothetical scenario: the teachers brain (P , LT ) attempts to interpret the world through the students eyes (V S). Such supervision encourages to produce visual representations that can be readily interpreted by the teachers language pathway, thereby enabling implicit visual knowledge transfer within the unified text-probability space. 3.2. Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference Loss The right part of Fig. 2 depicts the components of Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, which consists of two complementary branches: teacher-guided distillation term Lt and student-guided term Ls. The overall loss is defined as the combination of the two branches: LDBiLD = Lt + Ls. (7) Teacher-Guided Distillation. The teacher-guided component Lt transfers the teachers most confident knowledge to the student by focusing on its most informative logits. Instead of using fixed for all samples, we adopt the Kneedle algorithm [37] to adaptively determine kt for each instance based on the distribution of teacher logits. The dynamic top-k selection procedure can be described mathematically as follows. First, given the teacher logits zt R1N for DBiLD, let zt denote the corresponding logits sorted in descending order. We then normalize both their ranks and values to the range [0, 1] to prepare for knee-point detection, yielding: xi = , yi = zt zt min max zt zt min , = 1, . . . , N. (8) Next, we define reference line r(x) = 1 and compute the perpendicular distance di = (1 xi) yi between each normalized point (xi, yi) and the reference line. The rank index with the maximum distance is identified as the dynamic cutoff kt: kt = arg max di, (9) which represents the transition point where the distribution shifts from the information-rich region to the long-tail region. After obtaining the dynamic cutoff kt, we selected top-kt teacher-led logits zt led from zt: led = [zt zt , zt i2 , . . . , zt ikt ] R1kt . (10) Then, we create the corresponding student logits zs cor by selecting the student logit values at the corresponding indices [i1, i2, . . . , ikt]: cor = [zs zs i1 , zs i2 , . . . , zs ikt ] R1kt . (11) Internal pairwise differences are then computed to capture the relative ranking structure: led = [ zt dt im cor = [ zs ds im zt in zs in 1 < kt ] R1 kt(kt1) 1 < kt ] R1 kt(kt1) 2 2 , . (12) The difference vectors are further normalized into probability distributions via temperature-scaled softmax: pt led = ps cor = exp(dt led/τ ) (cid:80) kt(kt1) i=1 2 exp(dt cor/τ ) exp(ds led,i/τ ) (cid:80) kt(kt1) i= 2 exp(ds cor,i/τ ) , . (13) where τ is the temperature parameter controlling distribution sharpness. The teacher-guided loss is then computed using reverse KL divergence (RKL) to align the students distribution with the teachers: Lt = DRKL[pt led ps cor], (14) which distills knowledge from the high-confidence region of the teacher distribution through targeted focus on the most informative logits determined by kt. Student-Guided Distillation. As counterpart to the teacher-guided branch, the student-guided distillation component Ls performs the same bidirectional alignment process, but from the students perspective. Instead of using the teacher logits to identify the high-confidence region, we dynamically select the top-ks logits based on the student distribution, where ks is obtained via the same knee-point detection procedure applied to zs. This adaptive selection enables the student to verify its most confident predictions against the corresponding teacher logits. Analogously, we extract the top-ks student-led logits zs led which sorted in descending order along with the corresponding teacher logits zt cor at the same indices. Based on these, we can sequentially compute their internal pairwise differences ds cor as well as the probability distributions ps led and dt cor. The student-guided loss is then expressed as: led and pt Ls = DRKL[pt cor ps led], (15) which verifies the students most confident predictions against the teachers distribution, thereby complementing the teacher-guided branch and establishing bidirectional symmetric supervision between the two models. 4. Experiments 4.1. Setup Setting. To ensure fair comparison in Table 1, our setting largely inherits the training configuration of LLaVAKD [2], employing the pretrained SigLIP-B/14@384px [53] as the visual encoder, two-layer MLP with GELU activation as the projector and Qwen2.5 family models as the LLM backbone. For comparison with Align-KD [7], as shown in Table 2, we also conduct experiments adopting CLIP ViT-L/14 [36] as the visual encoder, LDPv2 [6] as the projector, and lighter Qwen2.5-1.5B backbone instead of the 1.7B LLM [5] used in the original Align-KD. We divide the training process into two stages: Pre-Training (PT) and Distilled Fine-Tuning (DFT). The PT stage uses LLaVA1.5558K [31] to establish visionlanguage alignment, while DFT leverages LLaVA-Mix-665K [31] for effective knowledge distillation. For both stages, the teacher and student models share the same overall architecture. Specifically, the 0.5B student model is distilled from teacher with Qwen2.5-3B LLM backbone, while the 1.5B student model is distilled from teacher with Qwen2.5-7B LLM backbone. The temperature parameter τ is setted as 3. Loss weights for LAlign and LVSD are consistently set to λ1 = 1.0 and λ2 = 1.0 in all experiments. Benchmarks. We evaluate Switch-KD on ten widely used multimodal benchmarks, organized into five categories [25]: (1) Perception & Understanding: MMB [32], MMBCN [32], and MME [51] evaluate general visuallanguage understanding, multilingual perception, and multimodal alignment capabilities. (2) Cognition & Reasoning: VQAv2 [8], GQA [13], ScienceQA [33], and MMMU [52] evaluate compositional reasoning and complex problem-solving abilities, reflecting higher-order cog- (3) OCR: TextVQA [39] assesses text-rich vinition. sual question answering, evaluating the ability to recognize and reason over textual content in images. (4) Hallucination: POPE [27] evaluates robustness against visual hallucinations and their consistency in visual grounding. (5) Domain-specific: VisWiz [10] targets real-world images taken by visually impaired users, evaluating the robustness of visual perception in domain-shifted scenarios. Compared Methods. We compare Switch-KD against recent lightweight VLMs, including TinyLLaVA [56], MiniGemini [28], SPHINX-Tiny [29] and MobileVLM [6], as well as distillation-based counterparts such as MoVEKD [3], LLaVA-MOD [38], LLaVA-KD [2] and Align-KD [7]. For completeness, we also compare against SOTA VLMs, including LLaVA-1.5 [31] and Qwen-VL [1]. 4.2. Benchmarked Results with the SoTAs As summarized in Table 1, Switch-KD consistently achieves leading or competitive performance across models of different scales.

sections.content: the large-scale group (3B parameters), both TinyLLaVA-7B and TinyLLaVA-3B deliver strong overall results. Switch-KD-1.5B attains comparable Avg7 scores (64.5 vs. 64.9 for TinyLLaVA-3B) with far fewer parameters, and notably surpasses the distilled MoVE-KD-v1.17B on key benchmarks such as MMBench (+4.0), VQAv2 (+1.5), and TextVQA (+0.7), underscoring its strong generalization capability. Short / 3.6M Long / 3.6M Short / 1.2M MobileLLaMA-1.7B MobileVLM V2 MobileLLaMA-1.7B Align-KD MobileLLaMA-1.7B MobileVLM V2 MobileLLaMA-1.7B Align-KD Qwen2.5-1.5B Qwen2.5-1.5B MobileVLM V2 Switch-KD 1246.3 1288.4 1289.2 1303.8 1365.9 1411.5 57.6 57. 55.9 57.5 65.6 68.4 55.1 58.9 59.0 60.1 58.0 61.9 63.2 66. 64.5 67.7 68.9 71.6 51.2 52.4 52.2 53.1 51.2 57.0 85.3 86. 86.1 87.0 84.2 87.5 62.4 - 64.4 2.0 63.7 - 65.1 1.4 - 66.0 69.5 3.5 Table 2. Performance comparison of Switch-KD with Align-KD. Using fewer training samples and lighter LLM backbone, Switch-KD achieves superior performance over the baseline. MMEP refers to MME Perception. Short and Long refer to two subsets with different maximum prompt lengths limitations. denotes the average improvement of the distillation method over its baseline. In the mid-scale group (1.52B parameters), SwitchKD-1.5B achieves leading performance over both distilled and non-distilled methods. Using similar LLM backbones, it outperforms recent distilled VLMs LLaVA-MoD and LLaVA-KD by 4.4 and 0.4 points in Avg7, respectively, while maintaining greater data efficiency. Compared with the TinyLLaVA-1.5B baseline trained with conventional PT-SFT scheme, Switch-KD achieves gains of 1.6 and 1.3 points in Avg7 and Avg10, respectively, demonstrating consistent improvements across benchmarks. In the small-scale group (<1.5B parameters), SwitchKD-0.5B records 4.0 points improvement in Avg7 over SPHINX-Tiny while using only 8% of its training data volume. It further outperforms LLaVA-MoD and LLaVA-KD of comparable scale by 3.7 and 1.1 points in Avg7, respectively. Consistent with the 1.5B results, Switch-KD0.5B also surpasses the TinyLLaVA baseline by 4.9 and 3.6 points in Avg7 and Avg10, respectively, highlighting its strength at lower model capacities. Table 2 demonstrates Switch-KDs cross-architecture transferability. While Align-KD improves the baseline by 2.0 and 1.4 points with short and long instruction datasets, Switch-KD achieves 3.5-point gain despite using lighter backbone (Qwen2.5-1.5B vs. MobileLLaMA-1.7B) and one-third the data (1.2M samples), highlighting its distillation efficiency and robustness. 4.3. Ablation Study and Analysis 4.3.1. Is Visual-Switch Distillation Effective? We assess the effectiveness of the visual-switch architecture through both quantitative and qualitative analyses. As shown in Table 3, we compare the distilled performance of

sections.title: POPE
sections.content: Avg10 TinyLLaVA-0.5B w/o Switch w/ Switch 61.5 63.1 66.8 58.9 60.8 63.5 54.2 57.3 57.8 74.8 77.8 79. 58.3 59.7 61.6 59.1 58.1 57.9 33.6 31.7 29.8 49.2 51.0 52.3 28.9 41.5 44.9 86.1 87.0 87. 56.5 58.8 60.1 Table 3. Ablation study on the visual-switch architecture. w/o Switch refers to knowledge distillation with the standard alignment pathway, whereas w/ Switch incorporates the proposed visual-switch distillation. the 0.5B student model with and without the switch mechanism. Visual-switch distillation consistently outperforms both the baseline and the standard distillation architecture, raising the average score from 58.8 to 60.1. Notable gains are observed on VQAv2 (+1.8), GQA (+1.9), TextVQA (+1.3), and especially VizWiz (+3.4), suggesting that the switch effectively transfers the teachers robustness to challenging visual conditions such as low light and motion blur. Furthermore, improvements in the Perception & Understanding ability (MME, MMB, and MMBCN) and Hallucination robustness (POPE) indicate that the visual-switch mechanism primarily enhances cross-modal alignment and visualsemantic representation during distillation. Figure 3 compares attention maps from the teacher, an SFT baseline, two distillation methods, and our Switch-KD. The teacher focuses on semantically critical regions (e.g., the intersection between wooden bridge and distant mountains), demonstrating strong visualsemantic understanding. While the SFT baseline approximates the teachers overall attention distribution, it fails to match the finegrained semantic selectivity in specific regions. In comparison, LLaVA-KD spreads attention almost uniformly across the image, lacking semantic focus, whereas Align-KD activates only partial semantic regions, further highlighting the limitations of modality-separated supervision under the frozen visual encoder strategy. By contrast, Switch-KD closely replicates the teachers attention pattern, attending to similar regions and achieving stronger visualsemantic alignment with the teacher. 4.3.2. What Is the Impact of DBiLD Loss? As shown in Table 4, we fix all other variables and adopt consistent standard distillation pipeline to examine the impact of different loss functions on performance. Using only the forward KL (FKL) [18] or reverse KL (RKL) [9] divergence yields the same average score of 58.3. Introducing the bidirectional difference loss into the FKL framework forms BiLD-FKL, which slightly raises the score to 58.4, confirming the effectiveness of bidirectional supervision. Building on this, replacing FKL with RKL in BiLD yields BiLDRKL, providing further 0.2 points gain and suggesting that emphasizing high-probability regions during optimization (a) Original (b) Teacher (c) SFT (d) LLaVA-KD (e) Align-KD (f) Switch-KD Figure 3. Visualization of attention maps. Switch-KD (f) aligns the students visual focus with the teacher (b), producing attention maps consistent with teacher semantics. : visual encoder parameters frozen during training phases. is beneficial. Finally, our proposed DBiLD-RKL integrates dynamic Top-k selection, reaching an average score of 58.8 and outperforming the FKL baseline by 0.5 and BiLD by 0.4, demonstrating the efficiency and robustness of our dynamic bidirectional distillation loss design. 4.3.3. Why Distill Only in Fine-Tuning? We further evaluate the impact of applying distillation at different stages of the training pipeline. As shown in Table 5, the baseline PTSFT follows the standard two-stage process of Pre-Training and Supervised Fine-Tuning. Introducing distillation during Pre-Training (DPTSFT) yields modest 0.9 points gain on Avg10, suggesting that earlystage supervision provides limited improvement. In contrast, distilling only during Fine-Tuning (PTDFT) brings a

sections.title: POPE
sections.content: FKL RKL BiLD-FKL BiLD-RKL DBiLD-FKL DBiLD-RKL 63.2 63.3 63.4 63.5 63.3 63.1 60.4 60.3 60.7 60.9 60.7 60.8 55.8 57.0 56.1 56.8 56.6 57.3 77.0 76.7 77.3 77.0 77.4 77.8 59.8 59.4 59.5 59.3 59.5 59. 58.1 58.5 58.1 58.0 58.4 58.1 32.3 32.4 31.3 31.0 31.3 31.7 49.5 49.8 50.3 51.2 51.2 51.0 39.9 39.0 40.3 41.5 40.9 41.5 86.6 86.7 86.7 86.3 86.9 87.0 Avg 58.3 58.3 58.4 58.6 58.6 58.8 Table 4. Ablation study of different distillation strategies. BiLD-FKL denotes the original BiLD loss using forward KL divergence, while BiLD-RKL replaces FKL with reverse KL divergence. DBiLD-FKL applies dynamic Top-k selection to BiLD-FKL, and DBiLD-RKL combines reverse KL divergence with dynamic Top-K selection as our final choice.

sections.title: Training Scheme
sections.content: PT-SFT DPT-SFT PT-DFT DPT-DFT Avg7 52.9 54.1 55.9 56.0 Avg10 56.5 57.4 58.8 58.7 Table 5. Ablation study on different training schemes. Teacher LLM Student LLM Avg7 Avg10 Qwen2.5-3B Qwen2.5-7B Qwen2.5-0.5B Qwen2.5-1.5B Qwen2.5-0.5B Qwen2.5-1.5B 57.8 63. 57.1 64.5 60.1 64.8 59.8 65.3 Table 6. Ablation study on teacher models with different sizes. larger 2.3 points gain, indicating that task-specific imitation of the teachers outputs facilitates more effective knowledge transfer. However, applying distillation at both stages (DPTDFT) results in slight 0.1 points drop, showing no additive benefit and confirming that the main improvement comes from replacing conventional SFT with DFT. To prioritize training efficiency, we avoid LLaVA-KDs three-stage framework (DPTSFTDFT), adopting instead streamlined two-stage pipeline that maximizes distillation performance while avoiding additional resource costs. 4.3.4. How Does Scaling Affect Distillation? In Table 6, we additionally analyze the impact of teacher model size on distillation performance under different teacherstudent configurations. When using Qwen2.5-3B as the teacher LLM, the student models achieve average scores of 60.1 and 64.8 for the 0.5B and 1.5B variants, respectively. Replacing the teacher LLM with larger Qwen2.5-7B yields further gains, where the 1.5B student reaches 65.3 on Avg10, surpassing its 3B-teacher counterpart by 0.5 points. However, for the 0.5B student, using stronger teacher causes slight performance drop (59.8 vs. 60.1), indicating that the benefits of larger teachers are constrained and can even be reversed by limited student capacity. This observation reinforces the overall finding that, while larger teachers provide better supervision, the effectiveness of distillation is ultimately bounded by the representational capacity of the student model. 4.3.5. Where Can Switch-KD Be Improved? Note that Switch-KD currently requires feature-space and vocabulary consistency between teacher and student for stable cross-modal knowledge transfer. While effective, this limits its applicability to heterogeneous architectures. Future work may investigate architecture-agnostic distillation or adapter-based mappings to relax this constraint, enabling knowledge transfer across diverse VLM designs. 5. Conclusion In this work, we introduce Switch-KD, unified visionlanguage knowledge distillation framework with two key components: visual-switch architecture and Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss. The visual-switch architecture switches the students visual outputs into the teachers language pathway, enabling implicit cross-modal supervision within shared textprobability space. The DBiLD loss adaptively selects the most informative top-k logits and performs bi-directional rank alignment via reverse KL divergence, focusing on high-confidence regions while preserving the teachers relative probability structure. Experiments on ten multimodal benchmarks show that Switch-KD consistently surpasses state-of-the-art distillation and lightweight VLM approaches. We believe it offers practical solution for deploying high-performance visionlanguage models in resource-constrained scenarios and may inspire future research on unified multimodal distillation.
